meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, jagat is the empirical world of name and form (nāma-rūpa) — real from the vyāvahārika (practical) standpoint, ultimately mithyā (apparent) from the pāramārthika (absolute) standpoint. The prayer protects both puruṣa (conscious beings) and jagat (the entire phenomenal world) — nothing should be excluded from Rudra's grace.

panini: P 3.2.178 — reduplicative formation from √gam: ga + gam + at → jagat (with P 6.1.9 reduplication rules)
P 7.1.70 — t-stem declension: nom./acc. sg. jagat

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 1.1 — listed among loka-vācaka (world-denoting) and gati-vācaka (motion-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.7 — जगत् listed meaning 'viśva' (universe): 'lokaḥ jagat viśvam'.

meanings.english: Auspicious; benign; gracious; kind; peaceful; propitious; the one who brings well-being
meanings.nirukta: Yāska derives śiva from √śī — 'śete sukham yasmin sa śivaḥ' (in whom/which one rests in comfort). Also parsed as 'śam + iva' — 'like peace itself'. The Nirukta tradition emphasizes that śiva is fundamentally a quality-word (guṇa-vācaka) before becoming a proper name — it describes the benign aspect of the same deity whose terrible form is 'Rudra'.
meanings.vedantic: The transition from 'Rudra' to 'Śiva' is the central theological movement of the Śrī Rudram — the terrible is asked to become auspicious. In Advaita, Śiva represents the nirguṇa Brahman beyond attributes, whose nature is inherently śānta (peaceful). Rudra's weapons becoming 'śiva' symbolizes the transformation of worldly suffering into spiritual awakening.

panini: P 5.3.55 — tamac-pratyaya for superlative: शिव + तम → शिवतम (most auspicious), fem. शिवतमा
P 4.1.4 — fem. ā-ending for adjectives: śiva → śivā
P 2.4.62 — as a proper name (Śiva), retains form in compounds

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.19 — listed among kalyāṇa-vācaka words (auspiciousness-denoting terms). Also in Nighantu 5.3 among deity-epithets.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — शिवः listed as one of the 27 names of the deity: 'śambhurīśaḥ paśupatiḥ śivaḥ'. Also Amarakośa 3.3.97 — śiva as an adjective meaning maṅgala (auspicious): 'śivaḥ śaṅkare cāpi kalyāṇe ca'.

meanings.english: Speech; word; voice; utterance; what is spoken
meanings.nirukta: Derived from √vac — 'ucyate iti vacaḥ' (that which is spoken). In mantra 5: 'śivena vacasā' — 'with auspicious speech'. The instrumental case indicates the medium through which the devotees approach Rudra.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedic theology, speech (vāc) is a divine power — Vāk Devī. 'Śivena vacasā' means approaching the divine with words that are themselves transformed into auspiciousness. The power of mantra lies in the word itself being a carrier of the sacred.

panini: √वच् + असुन् (Uṇādi 4.188) → वचस्
P 7.1.75 — s-stem (asanta) declension: instr. sg. वचसा

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 1.11 — listed among vāk-vācaka (speech-denoting) words: vacas, vāk, gir, uktha.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.6.1 — वचः listed in the Vāk-varga (speech section): 'vacaḥ vāṇī vāk gīḥ' — speech synonyms.

meanings.english: You (nominative); you (accusative, as tvā)
meanings.nirukta: The full nominative form 'tvam' is emphatic — 'you yourself'. In mantra 5: 'tvā giriśācchā vadāmasi' — 'we speak to you, O Giriśa, towards [you]'.
meanings.vedantic: The pronoun 'tvam' in 'tat tvam asi' (Chāndogya 6.8.7) is the jīvātman. Here it addresses Rudra directly, establishing the intimate I-Thou relationship of devotion.

panini: P 8.1.22 — त्वा is the enclitic accusative form
P 7.2.97 — nominative singular tvam

meanings.english: O mountain-lord; one who reclines/resides on the mountain; lord of Kailāsa
meanings.nirukta: Compound: giri + śa (from √śī 'to recline/sleep/rest'). 'Girau śete iti giriśaḥ' — one who reclines on the mountain. The epithet conveys sovereignty — the lord who rests upon the mountain as his throne.
meanings.vedantic: Giriśa connotes lordship over the earth/material world (pṛthivī-tattva). The mountain as throne represents sovereignty over all material existence. In mantra 5, the devotees address Rudra as Giriśa while declaring they speak to him with auspicious words — approaching the lord with śivena vacasā.

panini: P 3.2.15 — śa-pratyaya from √śī in upapada compound: giri + śa → giriśa
P 6.4.20 — dropping of ī before the suffix

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — गिरीशः listed explicitly among Śiva's names: 'giriśaḥ girīśaḥ'.

meanings.english: Towards; in the direction of; facing
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.9 — accha is a Vedic directional particle meaning 'towards, facing'. Used with verbs of speaking: 'acchā vadāmasi' = 'we speak towards [you]'. Rare in classical Sanskrit, common in Vedic.
meanings.vedantic: The directional particle emphasizes that the prayer is aimed at Rudra — not a general invocation but a focused, directed address.

panini: P 1.4.74 — accha classified among karmapravacanīya words that govern accusative
P 1.4.84 — accha as a gati (directional element) with verbs of motion/speech

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among gati-vācaka avyayas (directional particles).

meanings.english: We speak; we address; we utter; we declare
meanings.nirukta: √vad — to speak. 'Vadāmasi' = 'we address/declare'. The first person plural includes the entire worshipping community.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'śivena vacasā tvā giriśācchā vadāmasi' — 'with auspicious speech we address you, O Giriśa'. The act of Vedic recitation is itself a form of worship — the words, properly chanted, become the offering.

panini: P 3.4.78 — laṭ (present tense), uttama puruṣa (1st person), bahu-vacana (plural): वद् + मस् → वदामसि

meanings.english: In order that; so that; as; in such a way that
meanings.nirukta: Yāska treats yathā as expressing purpose or manner. In mantra 5: 'yathā naḥ sarvam ij jagat ayakṣmaṃ sumanā asat' — 'so that all this world of ours may be free from disease and of good mind'.
meanings.vedantic: Yathā introduces the desired outcome of the prayer — establishing the causal link between approaching Rudra with auspicious speech and the world becoming free of disease.

panini: P 5.3.23 — thāl-pratyaya from yad: yad + thā → yathā (in which manner, so that)

meanings.english: All; every; entire; whole; complete
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta treats sarva as expressing comprehensive inclusion — nothing is left out.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'sarvam ij jagat' — 'the entire world indeed'. The use of sarva with the emphatic particle it/id underscores that the prayer encompasses all of creation without exception.

panini: P 1.1.27 — sarva is classified as a sarvanāman (pronoun), hence takes pronominal declension
P 7.1.14 — sarva declines like a pronoun, not a regular adjective

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.1 — listed among words denoting totality/completeness.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.1 — सर्वः listed as a sarvanāman: 'sarvaḥ viśvaḥ ubhau' — sarva, viśva, and ubha are words for 'all'.

meanings.english: Indeed; verily; just; exactly; precisely (emphatic particle)
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 — Yāska classifies id as an avadhāraṇa-nipāta (restrictive/emphatic particle). In 'sarvam it jagat' it emphasizes: 'the entire world, indeed — without exception'.
meanings.vedantic: The particle id/it adds emphasis to sarva — 'all this world, truly all of it' should be free from disease. No part of creation is excluded from the prayer's scope.

panini: P 1.1.37 — classified as nipāta
P 8.3.15 — final d → t/ḥ in pause (id → it before consonants)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among avadharaṇārthaka avyayas (emphatic particles).

meanings.english: Free from disease; healthy; without consumption/wasting; without affliction
meanings.nirukta: Yakṣma is a general term for wasting disease (consumption/tuberculosis in later medical texts). Ayakṣma = absence of such disease. In Vedic context, yakṣma encompasses all debilitating illness — 'yat kṣīṇaṃ bhavati tat yakṣma' (that which causes wasting is yakṣma).
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'ayakṣmaṃ sumanā asat' — 'may [the world] be free from disease and of good mind'. Health of body (ayakṣma) and mind (sumanās) together constitute well-being. In Vedānta, the ultimate yakṣma is avidyā (ignorance) — freedom from spiritual disease is mokṣa.

panini: P 6.2.155 — nañ-tatpuruṣa: a + yakṣma → ayakṣma
P 2.2.6 — negative compound formation

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 2.9 — yakṣma listed among roga-vācaka (disease-denoting) words. Ayakṣma is its negation.

meanings.english: May it be; let it be; let there be; would be; may it become
meanings.nirukta: The imperative of √as. Expresses a benedictory wish — 'let this salutation exist, let it come into being.' The imperative mood elevates the salutation from description to performative speech act.
meanings.vedantic: 'Astu' transforms the salutation into a prayer — not merely stating 'salutation to you' but earnestly invoking 'may my salutation reach you.' This reflects the Vedic understanding that mantra efficacy depends on sankalpa (intention). In mantra 5, 'asat' (optative 'may it be') expresses the wish for the world to be free of disease.

panini: P 2.4.52 — √अस् is replaced by भू in certain lakaras (bhū-ādeśa)
P 3.4.86 — loṭ lakāra (imperative) formation → अस् + तु → अस्तु
P 7.1.35 — the tu-ending for 3rd person imperative
P 3.4.113 — liṅ lakāra (optative): अस् + ईत् → स्यात् / असत् (Vedic conditional)

meanings.english: He spoke forth; he declared/advocated; the intercessor; the one who speaks on behalf of
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: adhi (over/on behalf of) + √vac (to speak). Adhivaktā = one who speaks on behalf of others, an advocate/patron. Sāyaṇa: 'adhikṛtya vaktā' — one who speaks with authority. In mantra 6, Rudra is 'prathamaḥ daivyaḥ bhiṣak adhivaktā' — the first divine physician and advocate.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as adhivaktā is the divine advocate who intercedes on behalf of the suffering. As the first (prathama) divine physician (daivyo bhiṣak), he is the primal healer — establishing that healing is a divine function, not merely human.

panini: P 3.2.1 — agent noun formation: अधि + √वच् + तृच् → अधिवक्तृ, nom. अधिवक्ता
P 3.2.111 — luṅ (aorist): अ + अधि + वच् + त् → अध्यवोचत् (with vriddhi per P 7.2.3)

meanings.english: He spoke forth; he declared/advocated; the intercessor; the one who speaks on behalf of
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: adhi (over/on behalf of) + √vac (to speak). Adhivaktā = one who speaks on behalf of others, an advocate/patron. Sāyaṇa: 'adhikṛtya vaktā' — one who speaks with authority. In mantra 6, Rudra is 'prathamaḥ daivyaḥ bhiṣak adhivaktā' — the first divine physician and advocate.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as adhivaktā is the divine advocate who intercedes on behalf of the suffering. As the first (prathama) divine physician (daivyo bhiṣak), he is the primal healer — establishing that healing is a divine function, not merely human.

panini: P 3.2.1 — agent noun formation: अधि + √वच् + तृच् → अधिवक्तृ, nom. अधिवक्ता
P 3.2.111 — luṅ (aorist): अ + अधि + वच् + त् → अध्यवोचत् (with vriddhi per P 7.2.3)

meanings.english: First; foremost; primordial; chief; the original
meanings.nirukta: From √prath (to extend/spread) — 'prathate iti prathamaḥ' (that which extends first, the foremost). Establishes temporal and hierarchical priority.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 6: Rudra is 'prathamaḥ daivyo bhiṣak' — the first/primordial divine physician. This establishes Rudra-Śiva as the origin of healing — all medicine traces back to the divine healer. In the Śaiva tradition, Śiva is the Ādi-vaidya (primordial doctor) who cures the disease of saṃsāra.

panini: P 5.2.51 — thamac-pratyaya for ordinal: pra + thama → prathama
P 2.2.11 — ordinals in compounds

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.167 — प्रथमः listed meaning 'ādi' (beginning, first): 'prathamaḥ ādyaḥ pūrvaḥ'.

meanings.english: Divine; celestial; belonging to the gods; of divine origin
meanings.nirukta: From deva — 'devānām ayam iti daivyaḥ' (belonging to the devas). The vṛddhi suffix (-ya with āi) indicates derivation/belonging.
meanings.vedantic: Qualifies bhiṣak (physician) — the healing is not worldly but divine in nature. This is not human medicine but cosmic restoration.

panini: P 4.2.48 — yat-pratyaya with vṛddhi: देव → दैव → दैव्य (relating to/belonging to the devas)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 5.1-5 (Daivata-kāṇḍa) — extensive listing of deva-names, establishing the category 'daivya'.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.3.127 — दैव्यः listed meaning 'devata-sambandhi' (relating to deity).

meanings.english: Physician; healer; doctor; one who cures
meanings.nirukta: From √bhiṣ — 'bhiṣajyati cikitsati iti bhiṣak' (one who heals/treats). Yāska connects it to the concept of restoring wholeness.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as the primordial physician heals not just bodily disease but spiritual affliction. The Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad presents Rudra-Śiva as the ultimate healer of the disease of transmigration (bhava-roga). The Ayurvedic tradition traces its origin to Lord Śiva through Dhanvantari.

panini: P 8.2.36 — final j → k in pause: bhiṣaj → bhiṣak

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.9.1 — भिषक् listed as synonym of vaidya: 'bhiṣak vaidyaḥ cikitsakaḥ' — physician synonyms.

meanings.english: Serpent; snake; a class of serpent-demons; also metaphorically: diseases, afflictions
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 4.23 — 'ahīn sambhajayati' — related to supernatural serpent-beings. In Vedic, 'ahi' can mean both literal serpents and metaphorical forces of obstruction (cf. Vṛtra as the arch-ahi in Ṛgveda).
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 6, Rudra destroys 'ahīn' (serpent-demons) — representing forces of evil and disease. In Vedāntic symbolism, the serpent represents saṃsāra's coiling bondage — Rudra as divine physician destroys these binding forces.

panini: P 7.1.73 — i-stem declension: acc. pl. अहीन् (i → ī before n-ending)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 1.9 — listed among sarpa-vācaka (serpent-denoting) words: ahi, sarpa, nāga.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.8.6 — अहिः listed among sarpa-names: 'sarpaḥ pṛdākuḥ bhujagaḥ bhujaṅgaḥ ahiḥ' — serpent synonyms.

meanings.english: And; also; both; as well as
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 — ca is the primary samuccaya (additive) particle. Unlike 'uta' which has emphatic force, 'ca' is neutral conjunction.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedic syntax, ca typically follows the word it connects (postpositive). In mantra 6: 'ahīṃśca sarvān jambhayan sarvāṃśca yātudhānyaḥ' — 'destroying both all serpent-demons and all sorcerers'.

panini: P 1.1.37 — classified as nipāta
P 2.2.29 — ca as coordinator in dvandva compounds and serial constructions
P 8.1.63 — ca is always enclitic (anudātta)
grammatical_references.case_ending: indeclinable conjunction (always follows the word it connects)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.29 — listed among samuccayārthaka nipātas (conjunctive particles).

meanings.english: Crushing; destroying; snapping with jaws; devouring
meanings.nirukta: √jambh — 'jambhayati nāśayati' (to crush, to destroy with teeth/jaws). The causative form intensifies — 'causing destruction'. Related to jambha (jaw/tooth). Sāyaṇa glosses: 'nāśayan' (destroying).
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as the divine physician 'crushes' diseases and sorceries. The verb jambhayan (present participle) indicates ongoing action — Rudra continuously destroys evil forces, not just once but perpetually.

panini: P 3.1.26 — causative (ṇic): जम्भ् + णिच् → जम्भय
P 3.2.124 — present participle (śatṛ): जम्भय + शतृ → जम्भयन्

meanings.english: All; every; entire; whole; complete
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta treats sarva as expressing comprehensive inclusion — nothing is left out.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 5: 'sarvam ij jagat' — 'the entire world indeed'. The use of sarva with the emphatic particle it/id underscores that the prayer encompasses all of creation without exception.

panini: P 1.1.27 — sarva is classified as a sarvanāman (pronoun), hence takes pronominal declension
P 7.1.14 — sarva declines like a pronoun, not a regular adjective

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.1 — listed among words denoting totality/completeness.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.1 — सर्वः listed as a sarvanāman: 'sarvaḥ viśvaḥ ubhau' — sarva, viśva, and ubha are words for 'all'.

meanings.english: That one yonder; the one over there (pointing to something distant/visible but removed)
meanings.nirukta: Adas is the remote demonstrative — pointing to what is visible but distant. In mantras 7-8: 'asau yaḥ' — 'that one yonder who [is coppery, ruddy...]' — pointing to the visible sun, which is identified with Rudra.
meanings.vedantic: The remote demonstrative 'asau' in mantras 7-8 points to the sun — identifying the visible solar deity with Rudra. This is the Vedic technique of adhyātma-upāsana: using the visible (sun) as a gateway to contemplate the invisible (Brahman/Rudra). The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad uses similar pointing language for Brahman.

panini: P 1.1.27 — adas classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.107 — special suppletion: adas → asau (nom. sg. masc.)
P 7.2.102 — remote demonstrative declension paradigm

meanings.english: Copper-colored; coppery; reddish; the color of copper
meanings.nirukta: Named after the metal tāmra (copper) for its characteristic reddish hue. Describes Rudra in his solar form — the rising sun has a copper/red tinge.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 7, Rudra is described as tāmra (coppery), aruṇa (ruddy), and babhru (tawny) — the colors of the sun at different phases. This solar identification connects Rudra to the Āditya-principle (consciousness that illuminates). The Chāndogya Upaniṣad (1.6.6) describes the puruṣa in the sun as 'golden-bearded, golden all over' — Rudra's coppery hue is this same solar radiance.

panini: P 4.2.2 — tāmra as a color adjective derived from the material tāmra (copper)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 1.5 — color terms. Tāmra among lohita-vācaka (red-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.9.97 — ताम्रम् listed as a metal: 'tāmraṃ śulbam' (copper). As color: applied to objects of copper hue.

meanings.english: Ruddy; reddish-brown; dawn-colored; the color of the rising sun
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 12.16 — from √ṛ/ar — 'iyarti gacchati iti aruṇaḥ' (that which moves/rises). Refers to the ruddy glow of dawn/sunrise. Also the name of the charioteer of the sun (Sūrya).
meanings.vedantic: The dawn-color describes Rudra in his benevolent solar aspect — the sun that brings light after darkness. In Vedāntic meditation, the aruṇa-varṇa (dawn-colored) puruṣa in the sun is a form for upāsana of Brahman.

panini: Uṇādi 3.53 — √ṛ + uṇa → aruṇa

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 1.5 — listed among lohita-vācaka (red-denoting) words and also among sūrya-nāmāni (sun-names) in Nighantu 1.4.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.3.28 — अरुणः listed as a name for the dawn and the sun's charioteer: 'aruṇo'nūruḥ kāśyapaḥ'.

meanings.english: Tawny; brown; golden-brown; the color of a brown cow or dark honey
meanings.nirukta: A Vedic color term for the warm brown-gold spectrum. Often applied to Soma, cattle, and solar deities. Related to √bhṛ — 'that which bears/carries [warmth/color]'.
meanings.vedantic: The three colors — tāmra, aruṇa, babhru — describe the solar Rudra through his full color range from copper-red through ruddy to golden-brown. This threefold description mirrors the three states (avasthā-traya) or three guṇas manifest in the visible sun.

panini: Uṇādi 1.22 — reduplicative formation → babhru (u-stem)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 1.5 — listed among color-denoting words, specifically in the lohita/piṅgala (reddish-brown) category.

meanings.english: Very auspicious; most blessed; supremely propitious; embodiment of good fortune
meanings.nirukta: Compound: su (exceedingly) + maṅgala (auspiciousness). 'Suṣṭhu maṅgalam yasya saḥ' — one in whom auspiciousness abounds.
meanings.vedantic: After describing Rudra's fearsome colors (tāmra, aruṇa, babhru), the verse pivots to 'sumaṅgalaḥ' — supremely auspicious. This juxtaposition is the theological core: the terrible IS the auspicious. The colors of the setting/rising sun, which might seem fierce, are declared to be sumaṅgala.

panini: P 2.1.60 — karmadhāraya: su + maṅgala → sumaṅgala

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.19 — maṅgala listed among kalyāṇa-vācaka words. The su- prefix intensifies.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.4.25 — मङ्गलम् listed meaning 'puṇya' (merit/auspiciousness). Sumaṅgala is its intensified form.

meanings.english: Who; which; what; that which (relative pronoun forming correlative clauses)
meanings.nirukta: Yāska classifies yad among relative-correlative constructions fundamental to Vedic syntax. The relative clause structure 'yā te iṣuḥ ... tayā naḥ mṛḍaya' (which arrow of yours ... by that, be gracious to us) is a defining pattern of Vedic prayer — naming the fearsome and asking for its transformation.
meanings.vedantic: The relative pronoun 'yā/yad' in Vedic prayer introduces the item of fear which the devotee then asks the deity to make auspicious. This rhetorical structure embodies surrender — not denying the terrible but asking it to become gracious.

panini: P 1.1.27 — yad is classified as a sarvanāman (pronoun)
P 7.2.102-105 — declension paradigm for yad-śabda across genders
P 8.1.36 — ya-forms can be accent-bearing or enclitic depending on position

meanings.english: This; this here; this very (proximate demonstrative pointing to something nearby)
meanings.nirukta: Idam points to what is immediate and present, contrasted with adas (remote). In mantra 7: 'imāṃ rudrāḥ' — 'these Rudras here [in all directions]'.
meanings.vedantic: The proximate 'idam' (this) vs remote 'adas' (that) establishes a spatial theology — Rudra is both 'asau' (that one yonder — the transcendent sun) and manifest 'here' (imāṃ — in all directions through the Rudras). This is the saguṇa-nirguṇa polarity.

panini: P 1.1.27 — idam classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.108-109 — special suppletion forms: idam → imam/imām
P 7.2.85 — enad-ādeśa: idam → enam/enām (anaphoric replacement)

meanings.english: The Howler; the Terrible; the Remover of sorrow; the one who drives away suffering; the Red/Shining one
meanings.nirukta: Yāska (Nirukta 10.3) gives multiple etymologies: (1) 'rodayati iti rudraḥ' — He who makes enemies weep; (2) 'rut dravayati iti rudraḥ' — He who drives away suffering (rut = duḥkha, dravayati = causes to flow away); (3) 'raudīti yaḥ sa rudraḥ' — He who roars/howls. The Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa (6.1.3.7) derives from 'arudat' — the crying of Prajāpati's offspring who demanded a name. Nirukta also notes the interpretation 'rut + dra' where rut = disease/sorrow.
meanings.vedantic: In Advaita, Rudra is Brahman in the aspect of cosmic dissolution (saṃhāra-mūrti). Sāyaṇa glosses Rudra as 'duḥkhaṃ rut tad drāvayati iti rudraḥ'. In Śrī Rudra Bhāṣya attributed to Śaṅkara, Rudra is identified with the Supreme Ātman. Abhinavagupta reads Rudra as primal consciousness that dissolves duality. The Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad (3.2) declares 'eko hi rudro na dvitīyāya tasthur' — Rudra alone exists, there is no second.

panini: √रुद् + रक् (Uṇādi 2.22) → रुद्र — agent noun formation
P 2.4.62 — Rudra as a proper noun (saṃjñā-śabda) retains its form in compounds
P 1.2.38 — as a name of a deity, it follows devata-specific accent rules
grammatical_references.case_ending: vocative singular, masculine (also nom. sg. in other contexts)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 5.3 (Daivata-kāṇḍa) — listed among the names of deities of the middle region (antarikṣa-sthāna-devatā). Also appears in the enumeration of Ādityas and in storm-deity lists.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 (Svargavarga, Śiva-nāma) — रुद्रः listed among the principal names of Śiva: 'śambhurīśaḥ paśupatiḥ śivaḥ śūlī maheśvaraḥ / īśvaraḥ sarva īśānaḥ śaṅkaraś candraśekharaḥ' — Rudra is one of the 27 synonyms of Śiva given by Amara.

meanings.english: On all sides; all around; from every direction; everywhere around
meanings.nirukta: From abhi + tas-suffix meaning 'from the direction of'. Abhitaḥ = 'from all directions'. In mantra 7: Rudras are 'abhito dikṣu śritāḥ' — 'stationed in the directions all around'.
meanings.vedantic: The Rudras pervade all directions (dikṣu) — this omnipresence reflects Brahman's sarvagatatva (all-pervasiveness). Nothing and nowhere is outside Rudra's presence.

panini: P 5.3.7 — tasil-pratyaya from abhi: abhi + tas → abhitas (from/on all sides)

meanings.english: Direction; quarter; cardinal point; the compass directions
meanings.nirukta: From √diś — 'diśati darśayati iti diś' (that which points/shows). The directions are 'pointed out' by the sun's movement.
meanings.vedantic: The Rudras stationed in all directions represent the omnipresence of the divine. The Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad (3.9.4) associates directions with specific deities — here all directions belong to Rudra.

panini: P 8.2.36 — final ś → k in certain environments (before ṣu): diś → dik before ṣu
P 7.1.20 — loc. pl. of consonant-stem: diś + su → dikṣu

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.2.1 — दिक् listed with directional terms: 'diś kakubh āśā' — direction synonyms.

meanings.english: Stationed; resorted to; dependent upon; leaning on; established in
meanings.nirukta: √śri — 'śrayati āśrayati' (to resort to, to be established in). 'Dikṣu śritāḥ' — stationed in the directions. The past participle indicates established presence — the Rudras are permanently stationed, not merely passing through.
meanings.vedantic: The Rudras 'stationed in the directions' (dikṣu śritāḥ) represent the cosmic guardians — Rudra's presence is not confined to one location but pervades the entire spatial framework.

panini: P 3.2.102 — past participle (kta): श्रि + त → श्रित
P 7.1.17 — nom. pl. masc.: śritāḥ

meanings.english: By the thousands; in thousands; thousandfold
meanings.nirukta: From sahasra (thousand) + śas (distributive). Indicates the innumerable multitude of Rudras — they are not one but thousands, pervading all space.
meanings.vedantic: The 'thousands' of Rudras reflect the infinite manifestations of the one Brahman. Though Rudra is one ('eko hi rudro na dvitīyāya tasthur' — Śvet. Up. 3.2), his manifestations are countless. This is the Vedāntic teaching of the one appearing as many.

panini: P 5.4.42 — śas-pratyaya for distributive/multiplicative: sahasra + śas → sahasraśas (by thousands)

meanings.english: We propitiate away their anger; we pray to avert their wrath; we seek to pacify their hostility
meanings.nirukta: Heḍas from √hīḍ — 'hīḍyate kopayate' (to be angry with). 'Ava... īmahe' = 'we pray downward/away'. The compound construction means 'we pray to propitiate their anger away from us'.
meanings.vedantic: The prayer to avert the Rudras' heḍas (wrath) is a plea for the transformation of divine anger into grace. In Vedānta, this 'wrath' is the karmic consequence of ignorance — propitiating it is seeking liberation from the fruits of past actions.

panini: P 3.4.78 — laṭ ātmanepada 1st pl.: √ईम् + महिन् → ईमहे
हेडस्: √हीड् + असुन् (Uṇādi 4.188) → हेडस् (wrath)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 3.12 — heḍas listed among krodha-vācaka (anger-denoting) words.

meanings.english: He descends; he glides down; he moves downward (said of the sun setting or moving through the sky)
meanings.nirukta: Compound verb: ava (downward) + √sṛp (to creep/glide). Describes the sun's movement across the sky — gliding/traveling downward. Sāyaṇa: 'avagatim prāpnoti' — 'attains downward movement'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'asau yo'vasarpati' — 'that one yonder who descends/traverses' — describes Rudra as the sun moving through the sky. The descending sun is identified with Rudra — even in 'going down' the divine maintains sovereignty. In Vedānta, this descent parallels the avaroha (descent) of Brahman into manifestation.

panini: P 3.4.78 — laṭ: अव + सृप् + ति → अवसर्पति (with guṇa per P 7.3.84)

meanings.english: Blue-necked; blue-throated (epithet of Śiva/Rudra referring to his swallowing of the hālāhala poison)
meanings.nirukta: Bahuvrīhi: 'nīlā grīvā yasya saḥ' — one whose throat is blue/dark. The blue neck is explained by the Purāṇic narrative of Śiva swallowing the hālāhala (cosmic poison) during samudra-manthana (churning of the ocean) — his throat turned blue from holding the poison.
meanings.vedantic: The blue neck symbolizes Rudra's capacity to absorb the world's poison (suffering/evil) without being destroyed by it. In Vedānta, this represents the jñānī (knower of Brahman) who remains untouched by the world's afflictions even while present within it — like a lotus in water.

panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: nīla + grīvā → nīlagrīva (one whose neck is blue)
P 2.2.35 — the compound takes masculine gender matching the person described, not the final member

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Component: Nighantu 2.4 — grīvā listed among aṅga-nāmāni (body-part names). Nīla among varṇa-vācaka (color-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — नीलकण्ठः (nīlakaṇṭha — blue-throated) listed as one of Śiva's names. Grīvā/Kaṇṭha are synonyms: Amarakośa 2.6.92 — 'grīvā kaṇṭhaḥ' — neck synonyms.

meanings.english: Intensely red; very red; blood-red; crimson
meanings.nirukta: Vi- intensifies lohita (red): 'viśeṣeṇa lohitaḥ' — specially/intensely red. Describes the deep crimson of the setting sun or of Rudra's form. Lohita from √ruh/loh — related to 'rudhira' (blood).
meanings.vedantic: Vi-lohita (intensely red) describes Rudra-as-sun in full crimson glory. The vivid redness contrasts with the nīla (blue) of the neck — the deity encompasses the full spectrum. In Vedāntic symbolism, red represents rajas (activity/passion), blue represents sattva (purity) — Rudra transcends both while manifesting through both.

panini: P 2.2.18 — vi as an intensifying prefix with adjectives

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighantu 1.5 — lohita listed among rakta-vācaka (red-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.5.13 — लोहितः listed as a color: 'lohitaḥ raktaḥ' — red synonyms.

meanings.english: This; this here; this very (proximate demonstrative pointing to something nearby)
meanings.nirukta: Idam points to what is immediate and present, contrasted with adas (remote). In mantra 7: 'imāṃ rudrāḥ' — 'these Rudras here [in all directions]'.
meanings.vedantic: The proximate 'idam' (this) vs remote 'adas' (that) establishes a spatial theology — Rudra is both 'asau' (that one yonder — the transcendent sun) and manifest 'here' (imāṃ — in all directions through the Rudras). This is the saguṇa-nirguṇa polarity.

panini: P 1.1.27 — idam classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.108-109 — special suppletion forms: idam → imam/imām
P 7.2.85 — enad-ādeśa: idam → enam/enām (anaphoric replacement)

meanings.english: Cowherd; cattle-keeper; guardian; one who tends and protects
meanings.nirukta: Compound: go (cattle/earth/rays) + pa (from √pā, to protect). 'Gāḥ pālayati iti gopaḥ' — one who protects cattle. In Vedic usage, gopāḥ can mean 'protectors' in general.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'gopā adṛśan' — 'the cowherds saw [him]'. The cowherds represent ordinary people who perceive the divine in nature (the sun). This democratizes the vision — Rudra reveals himself not just to sages but to common herders. In Vaiṣṇava-Śaiva synthesis, the gopa theme connects to Kṛṣṇa-Gopāla.

panini: P 3.2.4 — upapada compound: go + √pā + ka → gopa (protector of cows)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 1.1 — go listed among pṛthivī-vācaka and dhana-vācaka words. Gopa as compound.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 2.9.56 — गोपालः listed as synonym: 'gopālaḥ gopaḥ' — cowherd terms.

meanings.english: To see; to behold; to perceive; to have the vision of
meanings.nirukta: √dṛś — 'paśyati darśayati' (to see, to show). In mantra 8: 'adṛśan' (they saw) is repeated — first by cowherds, then by water-bearers. The past participle 'dṛṣṭaḥ' means 'having been seen/perceived'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'sa dṛṣṭo mṛḍayāti naḥ' — 'he, having been seen, shows mercy to us'. The act of seeing (darśana) Rudra is itself salvific — his being perceived triggers his grace. This is the Vedāntic principle that the divine reveals itself (sākṣātkāra) and that revelation itself is liberating. Cf. Brahma Sūtra 1.1.2: 'janmādy asya yataḥ' — Brahman is known by its effects.

panini: P 3.2.111 — laṅ (imperfect): अ + दृश् + अन् → अदृशन् (Vedic form without guṇa)
P 3.2.102 — past participle: दृश् + क्त → दृष्ट (with ṣ per P 8.2.36)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.36 — √दृश् listed meaning 'paśyati' (to see). दृष्टिः (vision) in Amarakośa 2.6.54.

meanings.english: Water-carriers; those who draw/carry water; water-bearers
meanings.nirukta: From udaka (water) + √hṛ (to carry). 'Udakam harantīti udahāryaḥ' — those who carry water. Sāyaṇa identifies them as women going to fetch water from rivers — ordinary people encountering the divine in daily life.
meanings.vedantic: The water-carriers, like the cowherds, represent the common people who see Rudra (the sun) in their daily activities. The divine is not hidden from the ordinary — Rudra reveals himself to all, not just to the ritually initiated.

panini: P 3.1.118 — ṇyat-pratyaya: udaka + √hṛ + ya → udahārya (one who should carry water / water-carrier)

meanings.english: All; entire; universal; every; the whole; from all sides (as viśvataḥ)
meanings.nirukta: From √viś — 'viśati prāpnoti sarvam iti viśvam' (that which enters/pervades everything). Viśva denotes totality in an all-pervading sense.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'viśvā bhūtāni' — 'all beings'. The entire manifest creation sees Rudra-as-sun. Viśvataḥ (from all sides) in mantras 13 and 15 emphasizes comprehensive protection — Rudra's grace covers all directions.

panini: P 1.1.27 — viśva classified as sarvanāman
P 5.3.7 — tasil-pratyaya: viśva + tas → viśvatas (from all sides)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.1 — विश्वम् listed as synonym of sarva: 'sarvaḥ viśvaḥ'.

meanings.english: Beings; creatures; living entities; elements; that which has come into existence
meanings.nirukta: From √bhū — 'bhūtam = jātam prāptam' (that which has been born/come into being). Encompasses all living creatures.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, 'bhūta' refers to both living beings and the five elements (pañca-mahābhūta) from which the world is composed. 'Viśvā bhūtāni' — all beings/entities without exception perceive Rudra.

panini: P 3.2.102 — kta-pratyaya: भू + त → भूत
P 7.1.20 — neut. nom. pl.: bhūtāni

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Related: Nighantu 1.1 — bhūta related terms among prāṇi-vācaka (living-being-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.7 — bhūtam listed in dual sense: (1) prāṇī (living being), (2) mahābhūta (the five great elements: earth, water, fire, air, ether).

meanings.english: That; by that; him/her/it (demonstrative/correlative pronoun)
meanings.nirukta: Functions as the correlative partner to yad (relative pronoun). The construction 'yā te iṣuḥ ... tayā naḥ mṛḍaya' uses yā...tayā as relative-correlative pair.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedāntic usage, 'tad' famously refers to Brahman — 'tat tvam asi' (Chāndogya 6.8.7). The correlative use here points to the identity between the terrible and the auspicious.

panini: P 1.1.27 — tad classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.102 — declension paradigm for tad-śabda
P 7.1.25 — suppletion: nom. sg. masc. becomes saḥ (not tadaḥ)

meanings.english: To see; to behold; to perceive; to have the vision of
meanings.nirukta: √dṛś — 'paśyati darśayati' (to see, to show). In mantra 8: 'adṛśan' (they saw) is repeated — first by cowherds, then by water-bearers. The past participle 'dṛṣṭaḥ' means 'having been seen/perceived'.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 8: 'sa dṛṣṭo mṛḍayāti naḥ' — 'he, having been seen, shows mercy to us'. The act of seeing (darśana) Rudra is itself salvific — his being perceived triggers his grace. This is the Vedāntic principle that the divine reveals itself (sākṣātkāra) and that revelation itself is liberating. Cf. Brahma Sūtra 1.1.2: 'janmādy asya yataḥ' — Brahman is known by its effects.

panini: P 3.2.111 — laṅ (imperfect): अ + दृश् + अन् → अदृशन् (Vedic form without guṇa)
P 3.2.102 — past participle: दृश् + क्त → दृष्ट (with ṣ per P 8.2.36)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.36 — √दृश् listed meaning 'paśyati' (to see). दृष्टिः (vision) in Amarakośa 2.6.54.

meanings.english: Be gracious; show mercy; have compassion; make happy; delight us
meanings.nirukta: Yāska connects mṛḍ to the idea of 'softening' — 'mṛdnāti duḥkham' (grinds away suffering). Related to mṛdu (soft, gentle). The causative form mṛḍaya means 'cause to be happy/gentle' — a direct plea for Rudra to exercise his gracious nature.
meanings.vedantic: Mṛḍaya is the central prayer of Śrī Rudram — asking Rudra to be gracious. In the Śaiva tradition, mṛḍa is itself an epithet of Śiva (Mṛḍa = the gracious one). The plea transforms the hymn from a litany of fearsome attributes into a loving petition.

panini: P 3.1.26 — causative (ṇic) formation: मृड् + णिच् → मृडय (with guṇa)
P 3.4.87 — loṭ (imperative): मृडय + हि → मृडय (hi dropped after aya per Vedic usage)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.1.32 — मृडयति listed as a kriyā (verb) meaning 'sukhayati' (to make happy).

meanings.english: Blue-necked; blue-throated (epithet of Śiva/Rudra referring to his swallowing of the hālāhala poison)
meanings.nirukta: Bahuvrīhi: 'nīlā grīvā yasya saḥ' — one whose throat is blue/dark. The blue neck is explained by the Purāṇic narrative of Śiva swallowing the hālāhala (cosmic poison) during samudra-manthana (churning of the ocean) — his throat turned blue from holding the poison.
meanings.vedantic: The blue neck symbolizes Rudra's capacity to absorb the world's poison (suffering/evil) without being destroyed by it. In Vedānta, this represents the jñānī (knower of Brahman) who remains untouched by the world's afflictions even while present within it — like a lotus in water.

panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: nīla + grīvā → nīlagrīva (one whose neck is blue)
P 2.2.35 — the compound takes masculine gender matching the person described, not the final member

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Component: Nighantu 2.4 — grīvā listed among aṅga-nāmāni (body-part names). Nīla among varṇa-vācaka (color-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 — नीलकण्ठः (nīlakaṇṭha — blue-throated) listed as one of Śiva's names. Grīvā/Kaṇṭha are synonyms: Amarakośa 2.6.92 — 'grīvā kaṇṭhaḥ' — neck synonyms.

meanings.english: Thousand-eyed; omniscient; all-seeing; one who sees everything
meanings.nirukta: Bahuvrīhi: 'sahasram akṣāṇi yasya saḥ' — one who has a thousand eyes. 'Thousand' is Vedic idiom for 'countless/infinite'. The compound signifies omniscience — the ability to see all things simultaneously.
meanings.vedantic: The thousand eyes represent sarva-jñatva (omniscience) — one of the essential attributes of Brahman (Brahma Sūtra 1.1.2). In the Puruṣa Sūkta (RV 10.90.1): 'sahasrākṣaḥ sahasrapāt' — the cosmic Puruṣa has a thousand eyes and a thousand feet. Rudra as sahasrākṣa is identified with this cosmic Puruṣa.

panini: P 2.2.24 — bahuvrīhi: sahasra + akṣa → sahasrākṣa (one who has a thousand eyes)
P 6.1.87 — savarna-dīrgha sandhi: a + a → ā: sahasra + akṣa → sahasrākṣa

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Components: Nighantu 2.4 — akṣi/akṣa listed among netra-vācaka (eye-denoting) words.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.42 — सहस्राक्षः listed as an epithet of Indra. In Śiva context, it indicates the omniscience shared by all supreme deities.

meanings.english: The bountiful one; the generous giver; one who rains down gifts; the munificent
meanings.nirukta: From √mih — originally 'to rain/sprinkle'. Extended meaning: 'to shower gifts'. 'Mīḍhvān = dātā' — one who gives bountifully. The perfect participle form suggests one who has abundantly given — an established giver. Sāyaṇa: 'kāmānāṃ varṣitā' — one who rains desired things.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as mīḍhuṣa transforms from the terrifying to the bountiful — the same deity who wields arrows also showers blessings. In mantra 13, the superlative 'mīḍhuṣṭama' (O most bountiful) is the climactic address — the peak of praising Rudra's generosity.

panini: P 3.2.107 — kvasu-pratyaya (perfect participle): मिह् + क्वसु → मीढ्वस् (with cerebral ḍh per P 8.2.32)
P 5.3.55 — superlative: mīḍhuṣ + tama → mīḍhuṣṭama (most bountiful)

meanings.english: Attendants; warriors; companions; strong beings; Rudra's retinue (gaṇas)
meanings.nirukta: From sat (being/warrior) + van (possessive). 'Sattvaṃ balam yasya' — one who possesses strength/being. Sāyaṇa glosses: 'anucarāḥ' (attendants, followers). Refers to Rudra's gaṇas — the host of beings that accompany the Lord.
meanings.vedantic: In mantra 9: 'ye asya satvānaḥ' — 'those who are his attendants'. The devotee offers salutation not just to Rudra but to his entire retinue. In Śaiva theology, the gaṇas (attendants) represent the various forces and beings that serve the cosmic will of Śiva-Rudra.

panini: P 3.2.130 — vanip-pratyaya: sat + van → satvan (one who possesses being/strength)

meanings.english: I (first person singular nominative)
meanings.nirukta: The emphatic first-person pronoun. In mantra 9: 'ahaṃ tebhyo'karaṃ namaḥ' — 'I made salutation to them'. The shift from plural 'we' (naḥ) to singular 'I' (aham) is significant — the individual devotee personally commits to honoring Rudra's attendants.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedānta, 'aham' is analyzed extensively — it is the locus of ahaṅkāra (ego) but also of the Ātman. 'Aham brahmāsmi' (Bṛhadāraṇyaka 1.4.10). Here the devotee uses 'aham' in the act of surrender — the 'I' that bows is simultaneously transcended by the act of bowing.

panini: P 7.2.86 — nominative singular suppletion: asmad → aham

meanings.english: That; by that; him/her/it (demonstrative/correlative pronoun)
meanings.nirukta: Functions as the correlative partner to yad (relative pronoun). The construction 'yā te iṣuḥ ... tayā naḥ mṛḍaya' uses yā...tayā as relative-correlative pair.
meanings.vedantic: In Vedāntic usage, 'tad' famously refers to Brahman — 'tat tvam asi' (Chāndogya 6.8.7). The correlative use here points to the identity between the terrible and the auspicious.

panini: P 1.1.27 — tad classified as sarvanāman
P 7.2.102 — declension paradigm for tad-śabda
P 7.1.25 — suppletion: nom. sg. masc. becomes saḥ (not tadaḥ)

meanings.english: Make; do; render; perform
meanings.nirukta: The most fundamental action-verb. In mantra 4: 'śivāṃ giritra tāṃ kuru' — 'O mountain-protector, make that [arrow] auspicious'. The imperative 'kuru' is a direct petition.
meanings.vedantic: The prayer 'tāṃ kuru śivām' asks Rudra to transform his arrow's nature — this is the Vedic understanding that the divine can alter the fundamental character of reality through will. In Vedāntic terms, it is asking the cosmic agent to exercise his freedom (svātantrya) benevolently.

panini: P 3.4.87 — loṭ (imperative): कृ + हि → कुरु (with special stem per P 6.1.59)
P 3.2.111 — laṅ (imperfect): अ + कृ + अम् → अकरम्

meanings.english: Salutations; reverential bowing; surrender; homage
meanings.nirukta: Yāska (Nirukta 3.16) derives from √nam — 'namati praṇamati' (one bows down). Also parsed as 'na + maḥ' (not mine), indicating surrender of ego.
meanings.vedantic: In Advaita, 'namaḥ' is parsed as 'na mama' (not mine) — a surrender of kartṛtva and bhoktṛtva (doership and enjoyership). It represents complete effacement of ahaṅkāra before the Supreme.

panini: √नम् + असुन् (Uṇādi 4.188) → नमस्
P 8.3.34 — visarjanīya (ḥ) at pada-end before pause (avasāna)
Governs dative case (caturthī vibhakti) by Aṣṭādhyāyī 2.3.16: 'Namaḥ-svasti-svāhā-svadhā-alaṃ-vaṣaḍ-yogāc-ca'
grammatical_references.case_ending: nominative/accusative singular, neuter (treated as indeclinable avyaya)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighaṇṭu 3.18 — listed among karmavācaka words (action-denoting terms). Naighaṇṭuka-kāṇḍa groups it with acts of reverence.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.4.19 — listed as an avyaya indicating praṇāma (prostration) or reverence.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Anekārtha-kāṇḍa 5.26 lists it under Avyayas meaning bowing or salutation (namaskāra).

meanings.english: The commander of armies; general; leader of the hosts
meanings.nirukta: 'senāṃ nayati iti senānīḥ' — He who leads the armies/hosts.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra is the commander of all divine forces (deva-senā) that combat the forces of ignorance, suffering, and adharma.

panini: Upapada-samāsa: senā-śabda + √nī + kvip-pratyaya (P 3.2.61). Dative singular: senānye.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: senā is listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.17 among army/battle names.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Kṣatriyavarga (2.8.84) lists senānī as a synonym of commander (senāpati).
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Abhidhānaratnamālā 2.66 lists senānī as leader of the army.

meanings.english: Directions; quarters of space
meanings.nirukta: 'diśaty avakāśaṃ yā sā diś' — That which points out or provides space/directions.
meanings.vedantic: The directions are the spatial coordinates of the manifest universe. Rudra as 'diśāṃ patiḥ' (Lord of the directions) represents the omnipresent consciousness that pervades and rules all quarters of space.

panini: Root noun from √diś + kvip. Genitive plural: diśām.

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Svargavarga (1.1.58) lists diś as synonym of kakubha, āśā, and harit.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Abhidhānaratnamālā 1.24 lists diś among spatial synonyms.

meanings.english: Lord; master; husband; protector
meanings.nirukta: Derived from √pā: 'pāti iti patiḥ' — He who protects and preserves.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as Pati represents the Supreme Lord (Īśvara) of all creation. He is the master who rules over all individual souls (paśu) and bondages (pāśa).

panini: √पा + अति (Uṇādi 4.58) → पति — protector/lord
P 1.4.8 — pati-śabda when not compounded has the 'ghi' designation only optionally in case endings, but in compounds it behaves like regular i-stems.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with master or protector in Nighaṇṭu.

meanings.english: The counselor; adviser; possessor of sacred mantras
meanings.nirukta: Derived from mantra: 'mantrayate iti mantrī' — He who counsels or gives advice.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra is the source of all mantras (Mantra-draṣṭā) and the inner guide (Guru) who counsels the seeker.

panini: Derived from mantra + ini-pratyaya. Dative singular: mantriṇe.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: mantra is listed in Nighaṇṭu under speech/counsel synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Kṣatriyavarga (2.8.4) lists mantrī as synonym of dhīsaciva.

meanings.english: The merchant; trader
meanings.nirukta: 'vaṇijyate anena iti vāṇijaḥ' — He who is engaged in trade.
meanings.vedantic: Commerce represents the transaction of life, karma, and experience. Rudra as the merchant governs the cosmic law of karma-phala.

panini: Derived from vaṇij + a-pratyaya. Dative singular: vāṇijāya.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: vaṇij is listed in Nighaṇṭu under trade/exchange synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Vaiśyavarga (2.9.78) lists vaṇij and vāṇija.

meanings.english: Glades; thickets; hidden places; dry grasses
meanings.nirukta: 'kaṣati iti kakṣaḥ' — That which rubs against; or refers to hidden valleys.
meanings.vedantic: Represent the hidden or inaccessible parts of nature and the mind. Rudra is present even in the deepest thickets of the heart.

panini: Derived from √kaṣ + sa-pratyaya. Genitive plural: kakṣāṇām.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu under forest/grass synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa lists kakṣa under dry grass (2.4.156) and armpit/secret place (3.3.4).

meanings.english: One who spreads out or expands the earth/worlds
meanings.nirukta: 'bhuvaṃ bhūmiṃ tanoti vistārayati iti bhuvantiḥ' — He who causes expansion.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as the cosmic expanding force ensures that space and matter expand to accommodate life.

panini: Vedic derivative. Dative singular: bhuvantaye.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: √bhū is listed among growth and existence roots.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: bhuva is listed under earth/world synonyms.

meanings.english: Herbs; medicinal plants
meanings.nirukta: 'oṣaṃ duḥkhaṃ dahati yacchanti ca iti oṣadhiḥ' — That which burns away pain/disease.
meanings.vedantic: Herbs are the source of healing. Rudra as the Lord of herbs is the Ultimate Healer (Vaidyanātha) who cures all physical and spiritual diseases.

panini: Compound: oṣa + √dhā + ki-pratyaya. Genitive plural: oṣadhīnām.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.8 among plant names.

meanings.english: And; also; moreover
meanings.nirukta: Nirukta 1.4 discusses 'ca' as a nipāta representing aggregation (samuccaya).
meanings.vedantic: The particle 'ca' (and) is used to link the diverse aspects of the universe, reinforcing the Vedantic truth that everything is interconnected and ultimately one with Rudra.

panini: P 1.4.57 — classified as nipāta (avyaya)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 3.4.2 lists 'ca' under indeclinables denoting aggregation.

meanings.english: Foot-soldiers; infantry
meanings.nirukta: 'padbhyāṃ gacchatīti pattī' — One who travels on foot.
meanings.vedantic: Foot-soldiers represent the common, working classes. By saluting them, Rudra's presence is recognized in the humblest.

panini: Derived from pad + tini-pratyaya. Genitive plural: pattīnām.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: pattin is listed under army synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Kṣatriyavarga (2.8.80) lists pattī: 'pattis tu padātir padagaḥ'.

meanings.english: Running; chasing
meanings.nirukta: 'dhāvati iti dhāvan' — He who runs swiftly to protect or destroy.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra is dynamic, always moving and running to aid his devotees.

panini: Present participle from √dhāv + śatṛ-pratyaya. Dative singular: dhāvate.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: √dhāv is listed in Nighaṇṭu under movement roots.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Lists dhāv in the sense of running.

meanings.english: Living beings; devotees; strength/courage
meanings.nirukta: Derived from sat (existence): 'sato bhāvaḥ sattvam' — The state of being, or that which exists.
meanings.vedantic: Sattva represents the quality of purity and light. In the plural, it represents all living creatures that share the divine essence of Sat (Existence).

panini: Derived from sat + tva-pratyaya. Genitive plural: sattvānām (Vedic: sattvanām).

lexicographical_references.nighantu: sattva is associated with being or strength.

meanings.english: Trees
meanings.nirukta: 'vraścata iti vṛkṣaḥ' — That which is cut down; or 'vṛṇoti iti vṛkṣaḥ' — That which covers or provides shade.
meanings.vedantic: Trees represent the vegetative life of the cosmos. By saluting Rudra as trees, the Upanishadic seer recognizes the divine presence in all plant life.

panini: Derived from √vrasc + sa-pratyaya (Uṇādi 3.125) with samprasāraṇa.
grammatical_references.case_ending: dative plural: vṛkṣebhyaḥ (वृक्षेभ्यः) / genitive plural: vṛkṣāṇām (वृक्षाणाम्)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Vanausadhivarga (2.4.1) lists vṛkṣa: 'taruḥ śākhī palāśī ca dru vṛkṣo mahīruhaḥ'.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Abhidhānaratnamālā 2.1 lists vṛkṣa, taru, and mahīruha.

meanings.nirukta: Bahuvrīhi compound: 'haritaḥ keśāḥ (parṇāni) yeṣāṃ te' (those whose hair/leaves are green).
meanings.vedantic: The green leaves represent the life-giving, protective rituals (karma-kāṇḍa) of the Vedas that keep the tree of saṃsāra alive.

meanings.english: Cattle; beasts; animals; individual souls bound by worldly bonds
meanings.nirukta: 'paśyati iti paśuḥ' — That which sees; or 'pāśair baddhavyam iti paśuḥ' — That which is bound by a rope/bond.
meanings.vedantic: In Śaiva Siddhānta, 'paśu' represents the individual soul (jīva) which is bound by the bonds (pāśa) of ego, karma, and māyā. Rudra is Paśupati — the Lord who liberates these bound souls.

panini: Derived from √paś (to see/bind) + ku-pratyaya (Uṇādi 1.29).

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.11 under cattle/animal names.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Siṃhādivarga (2.5.2) lists paśu as synonym of tiryañc and catuṣpad.

meanings.english: Reddish-yellow; of the color of young, tender grass
meanings.nirukta: Compound: saspa (young grass) + piñjara (tawny/reddish-yellow). Represents the vibrant, golden-red hue of the rising sun.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra's form is compared to the beautiful, refreshing color of tender grass or the golden-red dawn, showing his nurturing and life-giving aspect.

panini: Karmadhāraya compound: saspa-sadṛśaḥ piñjaraḥ. Dative singular: saspinjarāya.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: piñjara is listed under yellow synonyms (Nighaṇṭu 1.15).
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa lists saspa under young grass (2.4.155) and piñjara under reddish-yellow (3.5.31).

meanings.english: Paths; ways
meanings.nirukta: 'panthānaḥ pathyante gamyante ebhiḥ iti' — Those that are walked upon.
meanings.vedantic: Paths represent both the physical roads and the spiritual paths (mārgas) like Devayāna and Pitṛyāna. Rudra as the Lord of paths guides the seekers along the right spiritual paths.

panini: Declined nominal stem pathin. Genitive plural: pathīnām (with Vedic vowel lengthening).

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.14 under path/way synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Bhūvarga (2.1.15) lists pathin: 'panthāḥ prathipathaḥ padavī sṛtiḥ'.

meanings.english: One who is seated on the bull; tawny-colored; golden-brown
meanings.nirukta: From babhru (tawny) + śa (possession/agent). Or derived from √bhṛ (to nourish/support): 'bharta-mūrtiḥ' — He who nourishes the worlds.
meanings.vedantic: Represents Rudra's form as the golden-brown or tawny sustainer of the universe, who supports all beings through his infinite grace.

panini: Derived from babhru + sa-pratyaya. Dative singular: babhlusāya.
grammatical_references.case_ending: dative singular: babhlusāya (बभ्लुशाय) / dative singular: babhlusāya (बभ्लुशाय)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: babhru is listed under gold/yellow synonyms (Nighaṇṭu 1.15).

meanings.english: Food; nourishment; matter; object of consumption
meanings.nirukta: 'adyate bhujyate iti annam' — That which is eaten by living beings.
meanings.vedantic: In the Taittirīya Upaniṣad, Anna is Brahman: 'annaṃ brahmeti vyajānāt'. Rudra as the Lord of foods is both the food (consumed object) and the consumer.

panini: Root √ad + kta-pratyaya (with ad -> an and t -> n by P 8.2.42). Genitive plural: annānām.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.7 under food synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Manuṣyavarga (2.7.26) lists anna: 'annaṃ bhaktaṃ surādhīnaṃ bhojanaṃ'.

meanings.english: One who wears the sacred thread (yajñopavīta) over the left shoulder
meanings.nirukta: 'upavītam asya asti iti' — He who is invested with the sacred thread.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra is the supreme performer of the cosmic sacrifice (yajña). Wearing the yajñopavīta shows his commitment to cosmic order (ṛta) and dharma.

panini: Derived from upa-√vye + kta → upavīta, then + ini-pratyaya (P 5.2.115). Dative singular: upavītine.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: upavīta is associated with sacrifice (yajña) in Nighaṇṭu 3.17.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Brāhmaṇavarga (2.7.41) lists yajñopavīta and upavīta.

meanings.english: Nourished; prosperous; full; complete
meanings.nirukta: 'puṣṭam asya saṃjātam' — That which is well-nourished or complete.
meanings.vedantic: Prosperity and strength are manifestations of the divine energy (vibhūti). Rudra as the Lord of the prosperous governs the distribution of abundance.

panini: Root √puṣ + kta-pratyaya. Genitive plural: puṣṭānām.
grammatical_references.case_ending: genitive plural: puṣṭānaḥ (पुष्टानाम्) / genitive plural: puṣṭānām (पुष्टानाम्)

lexicographical_references.nighantu: √puṣ is listed among growth and nourishment roots in Nighaṇṭu.

meanings.english: Existence; source of all being; Lord of creation
meanings.nirukta: 'bhavaty asmāt jagat iti bhavaḥ' — He from whom the universe comes into existence.
meanings.vedantic: Bhava represents the eternal source of existence. In Advaita, he is the substrate (adhiṣṭhāna) of all creation.

panini: Derived from √bhū + ghañ/ac-pratyaya (P 3.3.56). Genitive singular: bhavasya.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: bhū is listed among existence roots.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Svargavarga (1.1.30) lists bhava as a primary name of Śiva.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Abhidhānaratnamālā 1.15 lists bhava under names of Śiva.

meanings.english: Weapon; missile; stroke of energy
meanings.nirukta: 'hinoti hinasti vā iti hetiḥ' — That which is sent forth to strike or destroy.
meanings.vedantic: Represent the divine instrument of destruction of adharma. The weapon is the power of discrimination (viveka) that destroys ignorance.

panini: Root √hi + ktin-pratyaya (P 3.3.94). Dative singular: hetyai.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.20 under weapon/missile synonyms (āyudha-nāmāni).
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Kṣatriyavarga (2.8.91) lists heti as a synonym of weapon.

meanings.english: The moving world; the universe of changing objects
meanings.nirukta: 'gacchati iti jagat' — That which constantly moves and changes.
meanings.vedantic: The world is characterized by constant change (saṃsāra). Rudra as 'jagataṃ patiḥ' is the Lord and controller of this changing universe.

panini: Intensive participle form from √gam (jagamīti).

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 1.6 among world/earth synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Svargavarga (1.1.6) lists jagat: 'loko'yaṃ bharataṃ varṣaṃ jagat'.

meanings.english: The Howler; remover of sorrow; driver away of suffering
meanings.nirukta: Yāska (Nirukta 10.3) gives: (1) 'rodayati iti rudraḥ' (makes enemies weep); (2) 'rut dravayati' (drives away suffering).
meanings.vedantic: In Advaita, Rudra is Brahman in the aspect of cosmic dissolution (saṃhāra-mūrti), resolving all diversity back into primal unity.

panini: √रुद् + रक् (Uṇādi 2.22) → रुद्र.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Nighaṇṭu 5.3 listed among the names of deities of the middle region.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa 1.1.29 lists रुद्रः among primary names of Śiva.

meanings.english: One whose bow is drawn/stretched for protection; defender
meanings.nirukta: 'ātatena dhanuṣā avati rakṣati iti ātatāvī' — He who protects with a drawn bow.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra's readiness with his bow is to provide constant, immediate protection to those who surrender to him.

panini: Upapada-samāsa: ātata (drawn) + √av (to protect) + nini-pratyaya. Dative singular: ātatāvine.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: √av is listed in Nighaṇṭu among protection roots.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Standard lexicons list 'ātatāyī' (assailant), but the Vedic 'ātatāvin' specifically means protector.

meanings.english: Fields; sacred places; bodies (as fields of experience)
meanings.nirukta: 'kṣiyate nivāsyate asmin iti kṣetram' — That in which one dwells; or 'kṣatāt trāyate' (protects from decay).
meanings.vedantic: In Bhagavad Gītā (13.1), the body is called the field (kṣetra) and the soul is the knower of the field (kṣetrajña). Rudra is the Lord of both.

panini: Derived from √kṣi + tra-pratyaya (P 3.2.164). Genitive plural: kṣetrāṇām.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: kṣetra is listed in Nighaṇṭu 2.1 among dwelling/abode synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Bhūvarga (2.1.6) lists kṣetra as synonym of kedāra.

meanings.english: Charioteer; driver of the cosmic chariot; chronicler
meanings.nirukta: 'sūte prerayati aśvān iti sūtaḥ' — He who impels the horses.
meanings.vedantic: In the Upanishadic metaphor, the body is a chariot, the intellect is the charioteer (sūta). Rudra is the Supreme Charioteer guiding the soul.

panini: Derived from √sū + kta-pratyaya. Dative singular: sūtāya.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: sūta is listed under helper/charioteer synonyms.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Kṣatriyavarga (2.8.58) lists sūta as synonym of sārathi.

meanings.english: One who cannot be harmed/killed; inviolable; indestructible
meanings.nirukta: 'hantuṃ na yogyaḥ iti ahantyaḥ' — He who cannot be destroyed.
meanings.vedantic: Represents the eternal, imperishable nature (akṣara) of Brahman. The Self is never born and never dies.

panini: Negative Tatpuruṣa: na hantyaḥ. Dative singular: ahantyāya.
grammatical_references.case_ending: dative singular: ahantyāya (अहन्त्याय) / dative singular: ahantyaire (Vedic spelling)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Synonyms of imperishable are listed under Akṣaravarga.

meanings.english: Forests
meanings.nirukta: 'vanyate sevyate iti vanam' — That which is sought after or worshipped for peace.
meanings.vedantic: Forests are the abodes of silence and contemplation. Rudra as 'vanānāṃ patiḥ' dwells in these silent spaces.

panini: Derived from √van + ac-pratyaya. Genitive plural: vanānām.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 1.12 under water/abundance synonyms and 2.1 under dwelling.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Vanausadhivarga (2.4.1) lists vana: 'araṇyaṃ gahanaṃ kāntāraṃ vipinaṃ vanam'.

meanings.english: The red/shining one; of ruby-red color
meanings.nirukta: 'rohate vardhate iti rohitaḥ' — That which shines with a brilliant red color.
meanings.vedantic: The red color represents the active, creative force (rajas) or the fiery splendor of the sun.

panini: Derived from √ruh + itan-pratyaya (with h -> t). Dative singular: rohitāya.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed in Nighaṇṭu 1.15 under red synonyms.

meanings.english: Lord; master; husband; protector
meanings.nirukta: Derived from √pā: 'pāti iti patiḥ' — He who protects and preserves.
meanings.vedantic: Rudra as Pati represents the Supreme Lord (Īśvara) of all creation. He is the master who rules over all individual souls (paśu) and bondages (pāśa).

panini: √पा + अति (Uṇādi 4.58) → पति — protector/lord
P 1.4.8 — pati-śabda when not compounded has the 'ghi' designation only optionally in case endings, but in compounds it behaves like regular i-stems.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with master or protector in Nighaṇṭu.

meanings.english: Trees
meanings.nirukta: 'vraścata iti vṛkṣaḥ' — That which is cut down; or 'vṛṇoti iti vṛkṣaḥ' — That which covers or provides shade.
meanings.vedantic: Trees represent the vegetative life of the cosmos. By saluting Rudra as trees, the Upanishadic seer recognizes the divine presence in all plant life.

panini: Derived from √vrasc + sa-pratyaya (Uṇādi 3.125) with samprasāraṇa.
grammatical_references.case_ending: dative plural: vṛkṣebhyaḥ (वृक्षेभ्यः) / genitive plural: vṛkṣāṇām (वृक्षाणाम्)

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Amarakośa Vanausadhivarga (2.4.1) lists vṛkṣa: 'taruḥ śākhī palāśī ca dru vṛkṣo mahīruhaḥ'.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Abhidhānaratnamālā 2.1 lists vṛkṣa, taru, and mahīruha.

meanings.english: To the victorious one / one who tolerates / conqueror.
meanings.nirukta: From √sah (षहँ मर्षणे, Bhvādi-gaṇa, 1.0988) meaning to bear or conquer, with the śānac suffix (pres. participle) in the ātmanepada.
meanings.vedantic: Refers to the Lord who conquers all obstacles and tolerates the faults/sins of His devotees, transforming their hearts through patient corrective grace.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem सहमान + the dative singular case-ending 'ṅe' (caturthī-ekavacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). The 'ṅe' suffix is replaced by 'ya' after an 'a'-ending nominal stem by 'Geryaḥ' (P. 7.1.13), and the preceding 'a' is lengthened by 'Ato dīrgho yañi' (P. 7.3.101), yielding 'सह॑मानाय'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Related to terms for tolerance (kṣamā) and strength (sahatvam) in Amarakośa.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Associated with names of strength and tolerance in the Sāmānya-kāṇḍa.

meanings.english: To the deep piercer / shooter.
meanings.nirukta: Prefix 'ni' (deeply/down) + root √vyadh (व्यधँ ताडने, Divādi-gaṇa, 4.0078) + suffix 'ini' (possessive/agentive). One who strikes home or pierces thoroughly.
meanings.vedantic: The Lord's power of penetration that reaches the innermost recesses of the soul, destroying the core impurities and deep-rooted ignorance.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem निव्याधिन् + the dative singular case-ending 'ṅe' (caturthī-ekavacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). For '-in', '-i', '-u' or consonant stems, appropriate guna or sandhi rules apply. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with weaponry and piercing forces in Naighaṇṭuka-kāṇḍa.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Listed under weapon operators and archers in Kṣatriyavarga.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Associated with sharp weapons and piercing in Anekārtha-kāṇḍa.

meanings.english: Of the those who strike from all sides.
meanings.nirukta: See entry in Anuvakam 3. Prefix 'ā' + √vyadh + feminine suffix 'ṅīp'.
meanings.vedantic: The Lord's feminine energies of destruction that strike the ego and cleanse the soul from all directions.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem आव्याधिनी + the genitive plural case-ending 'ām' (ṣaṣṭhī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). The augment 'nuṭ' is added by 'Hrasvanadyāpo nuṭ' (P. 7.1.54) and vowel is lengthened by 'Nāmi' (P. 6.4.3), yielding final form.

meanings.english: To the those who are stringing their bows.
meanings.nirukta: Prefix 'ā' + √tan (तनुँ विस्तारे, Tanādi-gaṇa, 8.0001) + middle participle 'śānac'. Those engaged in stringing their bows.
meanings.vedantic: The preparation of the Lord's power. Stringing the bow represents the initial outward projection of the divine will (ikṣaṇa) prior to creation.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem आतन्वान + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). By P. 8.2.7 (na-lopaḥ prātipadikāntasya) if stem ends in 'n', the 'n' is dropped before 'bhyas'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to the word 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with stretching or expanding in Vedic glossaries.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Related to stretching and preparation in Vyāvahārikavarga.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified under active verbs in Anekārtha-kāṇḍa.

meanings.english: To the those who are fitting arrows to the string.
meanings.nirukta: Prefix 'prati' + √dhā (डुधाञ् धारणपोषणयोः, Juhotyādi-gaṇa, 3.0010) + middle participle 'śānac'. Those placing the arrow-head on the string.
meanings.vedantic: The immediate focusing of cosmic intent. Fitting the arrow represents the alignment of individual destiny (karma) with the divine law (dharma).

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem प्रतिदधान + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). By P. 8.2.7 (na-lopaḥ prātipadikāntasya) if stem ends in 'n', the 'n' is dropped before 'bhyas'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to the word 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with placing or fitting in Vedic registers.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Related to arrow alignment in Kṣatriyavarga.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified under active preparation verbs in Anekārtha-kāṇḍa.

meanings.english: To you all.
meanings.nirukta: The pronoun of address in the second person, used enclitically as 'vaḥ' in dative/genitive plural.
meanings.vedantic: Addresses the Divine directly as 'You' (Pratyagatman), indicating that God is not a remote third-person object, but the immediate, intimate subject of our own consciousness.

grammatical_references.panini: Enclitic/substituted form (ādeśa) of the second-person pronoun 'yuṣmad' (युष्मद्) in the dative plural by 'Bahuvacanasya vas-nasau' (P. 8.1.21).

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Pronouns are excluded from classical Naighaṇṭuka registers.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Noted under pronominal bases in Avyayavarga.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Pronouns are excluded from classical synonym tables.

meanings.english: To the salutations.
meanings.nirukta: Derived from the root 'nam' (नम्) meaning 'to bow, bend, or submit'. In Nirukta, 'namas' is defined in the sense of bowing or honoring (namanīya).
meanings.vedantic: Interpreted as 'Na' (not) + 'mama' (mine) — indicating the complete surrender of the individual ego and the realization that nothing belongs to the limited self, but all belongs to the Supreme consciousness.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem नमस् + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). By P. 8.2.7 (na-lopaḥ prātipadikāntasya) if stem ends in 'n', the 'n' is dropped before 'bhyas'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to the word 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed under water synonyms (udaka-nāmāni, Nighaṇṭu 1.12, item 17) and food synonyms (anna-nāmāni, Nighaṇṭu 2.7) due to its Vedic polysemous usage representing nourishment and flow.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Listed in Amarakośa Avyayavarga (3.4.19) as a term indicating bowing, salutation, or reverence.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified in Anekārtha-kāṇḍa (5.26) under Avyayas, meaning bowing or salutation (namaskāra).

meanings.english: To the salutations.
meanings.nirukta: Derived from the root 'nam' (नम्) meaning 'to bow, bend, or submit'. In Nirukta, 'namas' is defined in the sense of bowing or honoring (namanīya).
meanings.vedantic: Interpreted as 'Na' (not) + 'mama' (mine) — indicating the complete surrender of the individual ego and the realization that nothing belongs to the limited self, but all belongs to the Supreme consciousness.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem नमस् + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). By P. 8.2.7 (na-lopaḥ prātipadikāntasya) if stem ends in 'n', the 'n' is dropped before 'bhyas'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to the word 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Listed under water synonyms (udaka-nāmāni, Nighaṇṭu 1.12, item 17) and food synonyms (anna-nāmāni, Nighaṇṭu 2.7) due to its Vedic polysemous usage representing nourishment and flow.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Listed in Amarakośa Avyayavarga (3.4.19) as a term indicating bowing, salutation, or reverence.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified in Anekārtha-kāṇḍa (5.26) under Avyayas, meaning bowing or salutation (namaskāra).

meanings.english: To the salutations.
meanings.nirukta: Derived from the root 'nam' (नम्) meaning 'to bow, bend, or submit'. In Nirukta, 'namas' is defined in the sense of bowing or honoring (namanīya).
meanings.vedantic: Interpreted as 'Na' (not) + 'mama' (mine) — indicating the complete surrender of the individual ego and the realization that nothing belongs to the limited self, but all belongs to the Supreme consciousness.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem नमस् + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). meanings.vedantic: Interpreted as 'Na' (not) + 'mama' (mine) — indicating the complete surrender of the individual ego and the realization that nothing belongs to the limited self, but all belongs to the Supreme consciousness.

lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified in Anekārtha-kāṇḍa (5.26) under Avyayas, meaning bowing or salutation (namaskāra).

meanings.english: To the those who are sitting.
meanings.nirukta: From √ās (आसँ उपवेशने, Adādi-gaṇa, 2.0013) + middle participle 'ānac' (P. 7.2.83). Those who are seated.
meanings.vedantic: The state of stillness and meditation (Dhyana). The sitting form represents the immovable, changeless aspect of the Supreme Self (Purusha).

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem आसीन + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). By P. 8.2.7 (na-lopaḥ prātipadikāntasya) if stem ends in 'n', the 'n' is dropped before 'bhyas'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to the word 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with rest and sitting in Vedic glossaries.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified under resting postures in Sāmānya-kāṇḍa.

meanings.english: To the those who are lying down.
meanings.nirukta: From √śī (शीङ् स्वप्ने, Adādi-gaṇa, 2.0026) + middle participle 'śānac'. Those who are in a reclining or resting posture.
meanings.vedantic: Represents the latent, unmanifest state of the universe (Pralaya) and the state of deep sleep (sushupti) where all dualities dissolve.

grammatical_references.panini: Derived from the nominal stem शयान + the dative plural case-ending 'bhyas' (caturthī-bahuvacana) by 'Svaujasmauṭchasta...' (P. 4.1.2). By P. 8.2.7 (na-lopaḥ prātipadikāntasya) if stem ends in 'n', the 'n' is dropped before 'bhyas'. Governed by 'Namaḥ-svasti...' (P. 2.3.16) in relation to the word 'namaḥ'.

lexicographical_references.nighantu: Associated with sleep and rest in Vedic glossaries.
lexicographical_references.amara_kosha: Related to sleeping (śayana, svapna) in Manuṣyavarga.
lexicographical_references.abhidhana_ratnamala: Classified under sleeping postures in Sāmānya-kāṇḍa.

meanings.english: To the salutations.
meanings.nirukta: Derived from the root 'nam' (नम्) meaning 'to bow, bend, or submit'. In Nirukta, 'namas' is defined in the sense of bowing or honoring (namanīya).
